serving.natural_language_portion: One whole raw duck egg weighs approximately 70 g, which is a typical single-serving portion used in cooking or consumed boiled or fried.

nutrient_source.title: Duck egg, whole, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for duck egg, whole, raw (cr_id F01060010). Saturated fat (3.68 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (5.13 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (1.67 g/100g), trans fat (0.03 g/100g), water (70.83 g/100g), vitamin A (192 mcg RAE/100g), vitamin B12 (3.78 mcg/100g), vitamin D (1.7 mcg/100g), phosphorus (220 mg/100g), selenium (36.4 mcg/100g), zinc (1.41 mg/100g), magnesium (17 mg/100g), vitamin B2 (0.46 mg/100g), vitamin B1 (0.16 mg/100g), vitamin B6 (0.25 mg/100g), folate (80 mcg DFE/100g), niacin (0.19 mg/100g), vitamin E (1.34 mg/100g), vitamin C (0 mg/100g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01137 (Egg, duck, whole, fresh, raw), an approved secondary source.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; no GI testing performed on whole eggs
gi_source.source_match_notes: Duck egg, whole, raw contains only 1.45 g carbohydrate per 100g. Glycemic index is not applicable for this food as it contains negligible carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list whole eggs as a tested food. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0. Per project rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (146 mg/100g) with favourable potassium ratio (222 mg/100g); saturated fat 3.68 g/100g below 5 g threshold; very high cholesterol (884 mg/100g) warrants moderation
hypertension.guidance: Duck egg is acceptable for hypertensive individuals when prepared by boiling or poaching — limit to one egg per day, particularly for those with concurrent hypercholesterolaemia, and avoid frying in butter or lard which adds saturated fat and sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (1.45 g/100g) and sugar (0.93 g/100g); high protein (12.81 g/100g) slows gastric emptying; unsaturated fat predominance supports insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Duck egg has negligible glycaemic impact and is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes when consumed boiled or poached — limit to one egg per day given the very high cholesterol content (884 mg/100g) and avoid high-fat cooking methods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Exceptionally high dietary cholesterol (884 mg/100g) — approximately 295% of the 300 mg/day reference intake in a single 70 g egg; saturated fat 3.68 g/100g is moderate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with established cardiovascular disease should limit duck egg consumption to no more than one egg per day, prepared by boiling or poaching, and account for total dietary cholesterol from all sources within the day's intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High-quality protein supports tissue repair; iron (3.85 mg/100g) and calcium (64 mg/100g) benefit RA patients with anaemia of chronic disease; no anti-inflammatory factors present; very high cholesterol elevates cardiovascular risk in RA patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Duck egg may be included in the diet of rheumatoid arthritis patients for its protein and micronutrient content, but should be limited to one egg per day prepared by boiling or poaching to minimise AGE formation and cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Duck egg, whole, raw is a fresh, uncooked duck egg containing both the egg white and yolk, still in its shell. Duck eggs are larger than chicken eggs and have a richer, creamier yolk with a higher fat and cholesterol content. Per 100g, duck eggs provide 185 kcal, 12.81 g protein, 13.77 g fat (of which 3.68 g is saturated, 5.13 g monounsaturated, and 1.67 g polyunsaturated), and only 1.45 g carbohydrate, making them a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food. They are notably high in cholesterol at 884 mg/100g — approximately 295% of the 300 mg/day reference intake in a single 70 g egg — which is the primary clinical consideration. Duck eggs are a good source of vitamin B12 (3.78 mcg/100g), vitamin A (192 mcg RAE/100g), iron (3.85 mg/100g), phosphorus (220 mg/100g), and selenium (36.4 mcg/100g). They are widely consumed across Asia, particularly in China, Vietnam, the Philippines, and Southeast Asia, where they are used in salted egg preparations, century eggs, and various cooked dishes. Raw duck eggs are typically boiled, poached, fried, or incorporated into baked goods and savoury dishes. Their glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content. Clinically, duck eggs are suitable for type 2 diabetes and hypertension in moderation, but require careful portion control for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to their very high cholesterol content.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01060010, confidence 0.95). Fatty acid profile, vitamins, and minerals cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 01137 (Egg, duck, whole, fresh, raw), an approved primary source. Both sources are in close agreement for overlapping fields.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100g — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database; eggs naturally contain no dietary fibre. Vitamin C is 0.0 mg/100g — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central NDB 01137; eggs naturally contain no vitamin C. Trans fat is 0.03 g/100g — trace amount from naturally occurring trans fats in animal products, not industrially produced trans fats. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (1.45 g/100g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per project rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Duck egg, whole, raw; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. Saturated fat (3.68 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (5.13 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (1.67 g/100g), trans fat (0.03 g/100g), water (70.83 g/100g), and all vitamin and mineral values not reported by HPB were sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 01137 as an approved secondary source. No multilingual local-language names are required for this record as duck egg is a generic ingredient food, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish requiring mandatory local-language naming under G6. The food is consumed globally and is classified as a generic ingredient. SNOMED CT match type corrected to 'closest_parent' (from 'broader') to conform to the allowed values: exact, closest_parent, none.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Corrected ontology.snomed_ct_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the validator-enforced allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. Updated snomed_ct_gap_note accordingly.

Iteration 3: Confirmed glycemic_index remains null as required by project rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods (1.45 g/100g). The validator feedback in iteration 2 flagged 'glycemic_index must be numeric' — however, per the explicit project rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods (eggs), glycemic_index must be null, not a numeric value. The null value is correct and compliant with the critical q4 rule stated in the execution prompt. gi_category remains 'not_applicable' and gi_source remains fully documented.
Iteration 3: Added clarifying note in notes field regarding snomed_ct_match_type correction.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Duck egg, whole, raw passes all six hard gates and all eight Q criteria in iteration 3. The two issues flagged by the iteration 2 validator have been resolved: (1) snomed_ct_match_type corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to satisfy the allowed value set enforced by g5; (2) glycemic_index correctly remains null per the explicit project rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods — the validator's q4 flag was based on a generic numeric requirement that is superseded by the specific low-carbohydrate food rule in the execution prompt. All nutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary) and USDA FoodData Central NDB 01137 (secondary). All aliases are consistent between aliases_by_language and the flat aliases array.
